intro:
Hosea (Hebrew Hoshea, “salvation” — cognate with Joshua and Jesus), son of Beeri, was a prophet of the eighth century BC and the only writing prophet who was himself a native of the northern kingdom of Israel. He ministered through the reigns of Jeroboam II and the turbulent final decades of the North, down to the eve of Samaria’s fall to Assyria in 722 BC (Hosea 1:1). His message confronted a prosperous but spiritually adulterous nation steeped in Baal worship.

What makes Hosea’s prophecy unique is that God made the prophet’s own marriage a living parable. He was commanded, “Go, take to yourself a wife of whoredom… for the land commits great whoredom by forsaking the LORD” (Hosea 1:2). Hosea married Gomer, who proved unfaithful, and the names of their three children embodied the divine verdict: Jezreel (judgment to come), Lo-ruhamah (“no mercy”), and Lo-ammi (“not my people”) (Hosea 1:4-9). Yet Hosea was told to love his wayward wife again and buy her back from her degradation (Hosea 3:1-3) — a picture of the LORD’s redeeming love for faithless Israel.

The book moves between searing indictment and astonishing tenderness. Israel is charged with having “no knowledge of God” (Hosea 4:6) and offering hollow worship — “I desire steadfast love and not sacrifice, the knowledge of God rather than burnt offerings” (Hosea 6:6). They “sow the wind, and they shall reap the whirlwind” (Hosea 8:7). Yet God’s heart recoils from giving them up: “How can I give you up, O Ephraim?… My heart recoils within me; my compassion grows warm and tender” (Hosea 11:8). He promises to heal their apostasy and love them freely (Hosea 14:4).

Hosea’s reversals echo through the New Testament gospel. Matthew sees Israel’s exodus typology fulfilled in Christ — “Out of Egypt I called my son” (Hosea 11:1; Matthew 2:15) — and Paul and Peter cite the turning of “Not my people” into “sons of the living God” and the promise “I will have mercy on No Mercy” as fulfilled in the calling of all who believe, Jew and Gentile alike (Hosea 2:23; Romans 9:25-26; 1 Peter 2:10). Hosea thus stands as the great prophet of covenant love that judges sin yet refuses to let his people go.
